skill.name: azure-servicebus-py
skill.description: Guidance for using the Azure Service Bus Python SDK with queues, topics, subscriptions, sessions, scheduling, and dead-letter handling.
skill.summary: Build Python messaging workflows with Azure Service Bus queues, topics, subscriptions, sessions, and dead-letter queues.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many markdown backticks, weak-crypto fragments, and reconnaissance terms. Review found documentation examples for Azure Service Bus, with no malicious intent, prompt injection, data exfiltration, or unsafe dynamic execution. The only retained risk factor is a benign pip install command for official Azure packages.

low_findings.title: Benign Package Installation Command
low_findings.description: The bash block installs azure-servicebus and azure-identity with pip. This is normal setup guidance for the documented SDK and does not include shell metacharacters, remote scripts, or untrusted arguments.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is directly visible and limited to installing named Azure packages. It is executable guidance, but there is no evidence of command injection or malicious installation behavior.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: The static external command findings are primarily fenced code blocks and inline markdown identifiers. They document Python SDK usage and client method names, not Ruby backtick execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown tables or fenced Python examples. No Ruby code, shell interpolation, or agent instruction to execute arbitrary text is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Keyword False Positive
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings match ordinary words such as description and receive modes. No cryptographic algorithm, hashing routine, cipher choice, or security configuration appears at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text contains prose and parameter names, not cryptographic APIs. There is no evidence of DES, MD5, SHA1, or similar weak algorithm usage.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Keyword False Positive
low_findings.description: The system and network reconnaissance findings correspond to message processing examples, session identifiers, and timeout best practices. No host probing, port scanning, network discovery, or environment enumeration is shown.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are Azure Service Bus message handling examples. They do not instruct the agent to inspect the host, enumerate networks, or collect system details.

content.user_title: Build Azure Service Bus Messaging in Python
content.value_statement: Python teams need reliable patterns for Azure Service Bus messaging. This skill provides focused examples for queues, topics, subscriptions, sessions, and dead-letter handling.
actual_capabilities: Shows installation steps for azure-servicebus and azure-identity.
Provides async examples for sending, batching, and receiving queue messages.
Explains message settlement with complete, abandon, dead-letter, and defer actions.
Covers topics, subscriptions, sessions, scheduled messages, and dead-letter queues.
Includes a synchronous client example for simple scripts.
limitations: Does not create Azure resources or manage infrastructure automatically.
Does not include complete production retry, logging, or monitoring code.
Assumes the user already has Azure permissions and namespace details.
Does not validate live Service Bus configuration or access policies.

use_cases.title: Queue Worker Implementation
use_cases.description: Create a Python worker that receives queue messages, completes successful work, and abandons failed work for retry.

use_cases.title: Pub/Sub Integration Design
use_cases.description: Plan topic and subscription flows for services that need event fan-out and independent consumers.

use_cases.title: Operational Message Recovery
use_cases.description: Review dead-letter queue handling and scheduled message patterns for support and recovery workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Send a Queue Message
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a simple producer.
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to send one message to an Azure Service Bus queue using the Python SDK and DefaultAzureCredential.

prompt_templates.title: Receive and Settle Messages
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Python async receiver pattern that processes queue messages and uses complete, abandon, and dead-letter actions correctly.

prompt_templates.title: Design Topics and Subscriptions
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design an Azure Service Bus topic and subscription flow for three Python services with independent processing needs.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Production Pattern
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced messaging workflow with reliability concerns.
prompt_templates.prompt: Outline a production Azure Service Bus Python worker using sessions, batching, dead-letter handling, scheduling, and safe shutdown behavior.

output_examples.input: I need a Python queue sender for Azure Service Bus.
output_examples.output: A concise implementation plan with required packages, authentication setup, client creation, sender usage, and message batching notes.

output_examples.input: My worker must retry temporary failures and isolate poison messages.
output_examples.output: A receiver workflow that completes successful messages, abandons temporary failures, and sends permanent failures to the dead-letter queue.

output_examples.input: We need ordered processing for customer orders.
output_examples.output: A session-based design using stable session identifiers, session receivers, and guidance for preserving FIFO behavior.

best_practices: Use async clients for production workloads with high message volume.
Complete messages only after successful processing finishes.
Use dead-letter queues for poison messages and operational review.
anti_patterns: Do not use receive-and-delete mode when message loss is unacceptable.
Do not ignore max wait times in long-running receivers.
Do not complete a message before durable processing succeeds.

faq.question: Does this skill run Azure commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation and examples. Users must run any setup or Python code themselves.

faq.question: Which Python package does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers the azure-servicebus package and uses azure-identity for DefaultAzureCredential authentication.

faq.question: Does it support both queues and topics?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples cover queues, topics, subscriptions, and receivers for each pattern.

faq.question: Can it help with dead-letter queues?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows how to receive dead-lettered messages and complete them after review.

faq.question: Does it include synchronous and asynchronous examples?
faq.answer: Yes. Most examples use async clients, and a simple synchronous client example is included.

faq.question: Does it include Azure infrastructure provisioning?
faq.answer: No. It does not create namespaces, queues, topics, subscriptions, or access policies.
